workMixDesign.sourceDerivedSummary: Entry/junior residential architecture is dominated by digital drawing/model artifacts, cognitive coordination, and focused client/team communication. Site and field constraints matter, but this shorter student version handles them through team rules and a guided design studio instead of long writing or live calls.

sourceEvidence: O*NET includes site visits/inspections and construction coordination. [onet]
Junior site visit discussions emphasize observing, comparing to drawings, and escalating. [reddit:1c0xx7n]
physicalProceduralTool.simulatorImplication: Represent site conditions through Riley's verification reply and follow-on schematic constraints, not a physical playground.

sourceEvidence: O*NET lists CAD/BIM, drawings, specifications, email, and documentation tools. [onet]
Architect threads describe daily Revit/CAD production and redline pickup. [reddit:12eo2up][reddit:17zcdma][reddit:14b6qjh]
digitalToolArtifactWork.simulatorImplication: Most active scenes use realistic desk work surfaces.

sourceEvidence: Architects synthesize client needs, code, site, budget, and constructability. [reddit:15cyslv]
Junior mistakes come from editing without understanding downstream impacts. [reddit:12xw9j5]
cognitiveAnalysisDecision.simulatorImplication: Tasks require issue spotting and uncertainty handling.

sourceEvidence: O*NET emphasizes written documents and specifications. [onet]
Junior advice emphasizes organized notes and concise questions. [reddit:159xtnc][reddit:12p6n9p]
writtenDocumentationArtifact.simulatorImplication: Use a redline click board, a client-question checklist, read-only handoffs, and source tabs inside the design studio instead of long written prep.

sourceEvidence: Residential architects explain changes and tradeoffs to clients. [reddit:13yf1i4][reddit:1alviwh]
spokenInterpersonalCommunication.simulatorImplication: Represent the homeowner call as a deterministic Dana handoff so students can focus on question judgment and design moves.

sourceEvidence: Architecture days include email, meetings, redlines, and interruptions. [reddit:1hh974y][reddit:17mwbky]
passiveMonitoringWaitingContextSwitching.simulatorImplication: Use Slack-style handoffs and deadline pressure without adding extra waiting or live-call workload.

workMixDesign.simulatorPlan: Active scenes: Slack MCQ with redirects, A101/A201 redline click board, client-question checklist, Dana handoff briefing, and a Revit-like schematic option study.
workMixDesign.activeSceneMixRationale: The shortened flow keeps hands-on drawing judgment and design manipulation while reducing open-ended writing and removing the live voice call.

intro.title: Welcome to Harbor & Ash Studio
intro.content: You are a junior architectural designer supporting a residential addition permit package.
intro.imageBrief: First-person residential architecture studio desk with laptop dashboard, redlines, trace paper, plans, and material samples.

maple_street_inbox.title: Maple Street Inbox
maple_street_inbox.content: Maya gives you one short project summary before you choose your first move.
maple_street_inbox.imageBrief: First-person view at a residential architecture studio desk before redline pickup and homeowner call prep tasks. The scene shows trace paper, marked-up plans, a site survey sheet, zoning notes, a laptop with generic unreadable project-message shapes, pencils, and material samples. Keep the mood focused and realistic; do not render readable document text, emails, or answer text.

entryMessage.subject: Maple Street morning handoff
entryMessage.body: Please open the Maple Street project folder before you answer my first-move question. Use the brief, zoning notes, redline log, and site note to separate what is clear from what still needs checking.

files.name: 01_Client_Brief_Dana_Luis.md
files.previewTitle: Client Brief - Dana and Luis Moreno

sections.heading: Client goals
sections.body: Dana and Luis want a brighter kitchen, a useful mudroom for shoes and sports gear, and a second-floor bedroom suite.

sections.heading: Call timing
sections.body: Dana has a 9:30 call. Maya needs a careful first pass before anyone treats the model as final.

sections.heading: Rules to check
sections.body: Rear setback minimum is 25 ft. The permit worksheet should stay at or below 45% lot coverage.

sections.heading: Open issue
sections.body: A101 currently shows 24 ft 8 in at the rear yard and a 46% coverage worksheet note. Do not promise city approval from this file.

sections.heading: Known drawing fixes
sections.body: Window tags need coordination between A101, A201, and the window schedule.

sections.heading: Verify before changing
sections.body: Setback, lot coverage, privacy, and existing utility/site conditions need checking before design promises.

sections.heading: Right-rear condition
sections.body: The proposed addition is near a basement window and utility meter. The folder has a survey note, but the real site condition still needs field confirmation.

sections.heading: Maple tree
sections.body: Keep the maple tree protection area visible when studying the footprint.

coworkerRecap.fallback: I do not want to make up extra project facts here. Stick with the handoff I just gave you and keep the open items labeled.

turns.coworkerLine:
Dana and Luis own the house. They want to know if the rear addition can make the kitchen brighter, add a mudroom, and add a second-floor bedroom suite.

Before Dana's 9:30 call, Maya needs one careful first pass. Do not start designing yet. First, sort what is already clear and what still needs checking.

Watch for:
- zoning limits, like setback and lot coverage
- redlines in the drawing set
- privacy for the west bedroom window
- real site items, like the maple tree, basement window, and utility meter
answerFacts: Choose the best first move: compare client goals with zoning limits, redlines, drawing gaps, and questions for Maya or Dana.
The owners want a brighter kitchen, a mudroom, and a second-floor bedroom suite.
Maya needs a first pass before the model is treated as final.
The rear setback may be too short.
Lot coverage may be too high.
The west bedroom window needs a privacy decision.
The player should not sketch a fresh option first.
The player should not promise Dana that the setback is fine.
The player should not wait for Owen and Riley before writing anything.
Known facts should be separated from items that need verification.

first_move.title: Choose Your First Move
first_move.content:
Maya asks how you will use the first hour before Dana's 9:30 call.

The previous chat with Maya is available in View Briefing.
first_move.prompt: Pick the best Slack reply to Maya.

slackMessages.content: Dana's 9:30 client call is coming up. What are you doing first? I need judgment, not overconfidence.

options.label: I will sketch a better layout first so the call has a fresh design idea.
I will compare the client goals with zoning, redlines, drawing gaps, and questions for you or Dana.
I will tell the client the rear addition should be fine because the setback is basically at the minimum.
I will wait for Owen and Riley to answer everything before I write anything.

continuity: Later scenes reference this work where realistic.
deskWorkDesign.authenticityCheck: Visible source artifact -> student action -> workplace output -> rubric evidence.

redirect_sketch.title: Consequence: Design Too Soon
redirect_sketch.content: Maya redirects you: sketching before checking setbacks, lot coverage, and redlines creates rework.
redirect_sketch.imageBrief: Slack consequence scene in the studio showing Maya redirecting the junior architect.

redirect_constraints.title: Consequence: Useful First Pass
redirect_constraints.content: Maya agrees with the direction: separate clean drafting fixes from anything that needs verification before the Dana call.
redirect_constraints.imageBrief: Slack consequence scene in the studio showing Maya confirming the junior architect's careful first pass.

redirect_promise.title: Consequence: Overpromising Risk
redirect_promise.content: Maya stops you before the client hears that. The drawings still show setback and lot-coverage uncertainty.
redirect_promise.imageBrief: Slack consequence scene in the studio showing Maya redirecting the junior architect.

redirect_wait.title: Consequence: Waiting Stalls the Team
redirect_wait.content: Maya reminds you that incomplete information is normal. She wants a useful first pass with unknowns labeled.
redirect_wait.imageBrief: Slack consequence scene in the studio showing Maya redirecting the junior architect.

redline_note.title: Pick Up the A101/A201 Redlines
redline_note.content:
Mark 6 redline cards as Fix or Verify.
Use the Evidence to use box on each card. That is the only evidence you need for that card.
Choose Fix only for an obvious drawing problem when the evidence gives one exact drawing change.
Do not choose Fix just because a card shows a rule problem. If a setback, lot coverage, site condition, or client choice still needs a decision, choose Verify.
redline_note.inlineBriefingContent:
Dana and Luis want a brighter kitchen, a useful mudroom, and a second-floor bedroom suite.
Before Dana's 9:30 call, Maya needs a careful first pass. Do not start designing yet.
Your job here is to fix obvious drawing coordination problems, like wrong or missing window tags, and mark rule, site, or client-choice problems as Verify.
redline_note.decisionGuideTitle: How to decide Fix or Verify
redline_note.decisionGuideContent:
Use this guide for these six cards.

The Evidence to use box is enough. You do not need another hidden file.

Read in this order:
1. Read the card title and problem.
2. Read the Evidence to use box.
3. Ask: is this an obvious drawing coordination problem, or is it a rule, site, or client-choice problem?

Fix means: change an obvious drawing error now.
Choose Fix only for proven window-tag work:
- South-left window tag: the schedule says the opening is W-4, but A201 shows W-1. Change the A201 tag to W-4.
- Missing W-10 tag: the window is already drawn, and the schedule says the tag is W-10. Add the missing W-10 tag.
Do not choose Fix just because a measurement breaks a zoning or permit rule. A rule problem is usually Verify because the card does not tell you the safe design change.

Verify means: do not change the drawing yet.
Choose Verify when the evidence shows a problem but not one exact safe drawing change:
- Rear setback short: 24 ft 8 in is under the 25 ft rule. The team must check the dimension and zoning path before changing the footprint.
- Lot coverage over max: 46% is above the 45% rule. The team must check the worksheet and decide what changes.
- West bedroom privacy: there are possible solutions, but Dana has not chosen one yet.
- Existing utilities: the survey says utilities may be near the addition, but the real site has not been confirmed.

Final check:
Obvious drawing tag or label problem with one exact update = Fix.
Rule violation, permit math, site condition, or client choice = Verify.

categories.label: Fix
categories.description: The evidence gives one clear answer, so the drawing can be changed now.

categories.label: Verify
categories.description: The evidence still leaves a choice, site check, or rule problem, so someone must check first.

callouts.title: South-left window tag
callouts.text: A101 and A201 use different tags for the same south-left window.
callouts.sourceLine: Compare the drawing tag with this schedule excerpt.
callouts.owenNote: This is Fix. The schedule gives one exact tag: W-4. A201 shows W-1, so the drawing change is to update that A201 tag to W-4.

callouts.title: Missing W-10 tag
callouts.text: A201 shows the upper-left window, but its tag is missing.
callouts.sourceLine: Compare A201 with this schedule excerpt.

sourceRows.label: A201 drawing
sourceRows.value: Opening is shown, but no tag is shown

callouts.owenNote: This is Fix. The window is already drawn, and the schedule says its tag is W-10. Add the missing W-10 tag; do not redesign the window.

callouts.title: Rear setback short
callouts.text: A101 rear yard dimension reads 24 ft 8 in; local zoning requires a 25 ft minimum rear setback.
callouts.sourceLine: A101 says the rear yard is 24 ft 8 in. The zoning rule says the minimum is 25 ft.
callouts.owenNote: This is Verify. Even though 24 ft 8 in is short of the 25 ft rule, the card does not give one safe drawing change. The team must check the dimension and zoning path first.

callouts.title: Lot coverage over max
callouts.text: Permit worksheet shows lot coverage at 46% before deck revision; zoning max is 45%.
callouts.sourceLine: The permit worksheet says lot coverage is 46%. The zoning max is 45%.
callouts.owenNote: This is Verify. The 46% number is over the 45% rule, but that does not tell you which area to change. The team must check the worksheet and decide before updating the model.

callouts.title: West bedroom privacy
callouts.text: A201 west bedroom window needs a privacy strategy. Possible solutions include a higher sill, privacy glazing, exterior screening, or a smaller window, but Dana has not chosen one yet.
callouts.sourceLine: A201 lists possible privacy options, but Dana has not chosen one yet.
callouts.owenNote: This is Verify. The card lists possible privacy options, but Dana has not picked one. Ask Dana about the daylight and privacy tradeoff before changing the elevation.

callouts.title: Proposed addition area near existing utilities
callouts.text: The proposed addition area is close to an existing basement window and utility meter. The folder has a survey note, but no field confirmation.
callouts.sourceLine: The survey note says utilities may be near the addition, but the real site has not been confirmed yet.
callouts.owenNote: This is Verify. The survey only says utilities may be near the addition. Someone must check the real site before the team moves ahead with that footprint.

redline_note.reviewerIntro: I checked your pickup. Use one rule: Fix is for an obvious drawing problem with one exact update. Verify is for a rule problem, permit math, site condition, or client choice that still needs a decision.

deskWorkDesign.studentAction: Classify each redline as a fix that can be drafted now or a verification item that should not be edited yet.
continuity: Later scenes reference this work where realistic.
deskWorkDesign.authenticityCheck: Visible source artifact -> student action -> workplace output -> rubric evidence.

client_prep.title: Build Dana Question Checklist
client_prep.content: Before Maya talks with Dana, choose the two Dana-facing questions that would help the design team. Keep zoning, drawing-tag, and field-condition items as team checks.
client_prep.prompt: Choose exactly 2 Dana questions: one about privacy/daylight and one about the kitchen, mudroom, or daily routine.

appTabs.label: Project Notes
appTabs.heading: Maya's pre-call notes
appTabs.content:
Dana and Luis priorities:
- Brighter kitchen, without growing beyond the current footprint.
- Useful mudroom storage for shoes, coats, and sports gear without crowding the kitchen path.
- Daily routine concerns, especially kitchen access during construction.

Open redline notes:
- A101 rear yard reads 24 ft 8 in; zoning minimum is 25 ft.
- Permit worksheet shows 46% lot coverage; zoning max is 45%.
- A201 west bedroom window still needs a privacy/daylight choice.
- A201 window-tag fix is already proven by the schedule.
- Basement window and utility meter need field confirmation.

options.label: Which privacy choice feels best for the west bedroom window while still keeping daylight?
options.why: Dana needs to choose the privacy/daylight tradeoff.

options.label: What matters most near the kitchen: brighter cooking space, mudroom storage, or keeping the path open?
options.why: This helps the team place the kitchen and mudroom without guessing.

options.label: During construction, what daily routine or kitchen access should the team protect most?
options.why: This helps the team plan around the homeowner's day-to-day needs.

options.label: Can you approve the 25 ft rear setback if we move the addition?
options.teamCheckReason: The team must verify zoning and drawing dimensions. Do not ask Dana to approve a rule.

options.label: Can you confirm whether 46% lot coverage is allowed?
options.teamCheckReason: Lot coverage is a zoning and permit worksheet check for the team.

options.label: Should we change the A201 window tag from W-1 to W-4?
options.teamCheckReason: The schedule already proves this drawing fix. Dana does not need to decide it.

options.label: Is the basement window and utility meter clear in the field?
options.teamCheckReason: Riley or the project team must check the real site condition.

client_prep.successFeedback: Good. These questions ask Dana about choices only. The team will handle zoning, drawing tags, and field checks.

deskWorkDesign.studentAction: Select two homeowner-facing questions and leave technical checks with the team.
continuity: Dana handoff uses the same client-choice topics before the design studio.
deskWorkDesign.authenticityCheck: Visible project notes -> guided client questions -> deterministic Dana handoff -> design constraints.

client_prep.referenceContent: Ask Dana about choices and priorities only. Keep zoning, field checks, drawing tags, and permit math with Maya, Owen, or Riley.

client_call.title: Dana Call Notes
client_call.content: Maya already talked with Dana. Read what Dana decided. Next you will use Dana's choices and the team rules in the design studio.
client_call.referenceContent:
Use Dana's answers when you build the next design study.

Dana chose:
- West bedroom privacy: use Translucent glass or privacy glazing. The goal is to let daylight in while making the bedroom harder to see into.
- Kitchen and mudroom: keep the path through the kitchen open. Make the mudroom useful for shoes, coats, and sports gear.
- Daily routine: protect kitchen access during construction. Do not promise timing or cost yet.

The design studio also has Team Rules for the 25 ft rear setback, 45% lot coverage limit, right-rear utility clear zone, and window-tag fixes.

Next: use Dana's choices and the team rules to make the design option. Do not call the option city-approved or ready to build.

deskWorkDesign.studentAction: Read Dana's deterministic handoff before designing.
continuity: The design studio carries forward Dana's privacy/daylight and daily-routine priorities.
deskWorkDesign.authenticityCheck: Guided client questions -> client answers -> schematic design constraints.

schematic_option_study.title: Create the Maple Street Early Design Option
schematic_option_study.content:
Maya needs a quick early design option. Dana Choices tells you what the client wants. Team Rules tells you the limits the drawing must follow.

You do not need outside knowledge. Everything you need is in Dana Choices, Team Rules, and the Rules Check panel.
schematic_option_study.prompt:
Follow these steps:
1. Open Dana Choices.
2. Open Team Rules.
3. Return to Design Tool.
4. Fix the footprint until Rules Check is clear.
5. Add room labels.
6. Open West Elevation and choose Translucent glass.
7. Submit when Your Checklist is complete.

inputArtifacts.label: Team Rules
inputArtifacts.whereVisible: Team Rules reference tab, Rules Check, and drawing labels

inputArtifacts.label: Dana Handoff
inputArtifacts.whereVisible: Dana Handoff reference tab and west-window choice

inputArtifacts.label: Revit project views
inputArtifacts.whereVisible: Design Tool tab with Level 1, Level 2, and West Elevation views

deskWorkDesign.studentAction: Create a bounded early design option by adjusting a plan footprint, placing Level 1 and Level 2 room labels, choosing Translucent glass in the West Elevation view, and checking rules.

outputArtifact.label: Maple Street early design option study

continuity: The final assessment uses this as the concrete design-production artifact for the day.
deskWorkDesign.authenticityCheck: Visible constraints and plan canvas -> student design moves -> schematic option artifact -> rubric evidence.

referenceTabs.label: Dana Choices
referenceTabs.heading: Dana's choices to use
referenceTabs.content:
Dana chose:
- West bedroom privacy: use Translucent glass or privacy glazing so daylight still reaches the room while the bedroom is harder to see into.
- Kitchen and mudroom: keep the kitchen path open, and use the mudroom for shoes, coats, and sports gear.
- Daily routine: protect kitchen access during construction. Do not promise timing or cost.

Why this matters:
Dana wants privacy, but still wants daylight in the bedroom.

Use Team Rules for setback, lot coverage, the right-rear utility clear zone, and window-tag fixes.

referenceTabs.label: Team Rules
referenceTabs.heading: Rules for this design study
referenceTabs.content:
Use these as rules for today's schematic option, not final city approval:

- Keep the rear setback at 25 ft minimum.
- Keep lot coverage at or below 45%.
- Keep the right-rear utility clear zone open.
- Window-tag fixes are drawing fixes that the drawings already prove.
- Do not describe the option as city-approved or construction-ready.

assessment_gate.title: Studio Day Complete
assessment_gate.content: The Maple Street workday is complete. Your work is ready for assessment.
assessment_gate.imageBrief: End-of-day architecture studio desk with a marked-up schematic option, redline sheet, permit checklist, and constraint notes.

grading.title: Assessment
grading.content: Your work will be assessed against the junior residential architect rubric.

final_report.title: Final Report
final_report.content: Review your strengths and next steps as a junior residential architect.

maya_chen.name: Maya Chen
maya_chen.persona: Licensed project architect, calm and direct, protective of technical accuracy. She expects focused questions, careful uncertainty handling, and constraint-aware schematic design moves.

owen_reed.name: Owen Reed
owen_reed.persona: Practical Revit and drawing-set lead who appreciates exact sheet, tag, and detail references.

dana_moreno.name: Dana Moreno
dana_moreno.persona: Budget-conscious homeowner who wants daylight, privacy, and clear next steps without jargon.

riley_patel.name: Riley Patel
riley_patel.persona: Concise field lead who wants the design team to clarify drawings before construction proceeds.

intro.welcomeBody: Spend a day as a junior architectural designer supporting a residential addition at a mid-size studio.

steps.label: Read
steps.content: Get Maya's short handoff, then sort what is clear and what still needs checking.

steps.label: Choose
steps.content: Pick two client questions from a checklist.

steps.label: Design
steps.content: Use the Revit-like workspace to make a schematic option that follows Dana's choices and the team rules.

glossary.redline: A note on a drawing that shows something to fix, check, or explain.
glossary.permit set: The group of drawings and documents sent to the city for review before building work can begin.
glossary.permit package: The group of drawings and forms prepared for city review before building work can begin.
glossary.setback: The required open space between a building and the property line.
glossary.lot coverage: How much of the land is covered by buildings or similar structures.
glossary.revit: A building design program used to make 3D models and drawing sheets.
glossary.RFI: Request for Information; a formal question that asks someone to clarify a detail.
glossary.verify: Check with the right person or source before changing the drawing.
glossary.fix: A change you can make now because the evidence gives one clear answer.
glossary.zoning: City rules that control what can be built on a property.
glossary.constraint: A rule or limit the design must follow.
glossary.coordination: Making sure drawings, notes, people, and decisions all match.
glossary.drawing set: A group of drawings for the same project.
glossary.window tag: A short label, like W-4, that connects a window on a drawing to its details in the schedule.
glossary.schedule: A table that lists building parts, such as windows, and their details.
glossary.elevation: A flat drawing of one outside face of a building.
glossary.West Elevation: The drawing that shows the west side of the house from the outside.
glossary.footprint: The outline of the building area on the site plan.
glossary.schematic option: An early design idea used to test a layout before final drawings.
glossary.field condition: Something that must be checked at the real site, not only on the drawing.
glossary.utility meter: A device on a building that measures services such as gas or electricity.
glossary.clear zone: An area that must stay open and not be covered by the new addition.
glossary.permit worksheet: A form used to check project numbers before sending drawings to the city.
glossary.mudroom: A small entry area for shoes, coats, bags, and sports gear.
glossary.translucent glass: Glass that lets light through but makes details hard to see.
glossary.privacy glazing: Glass chosen to protect privacy while still allowing some light.
glossary.higher sill: A window bottom placed higher on the wall for more privacy.
glossary.exterior screening: A screen or planting outside a window that blocks views.
glossary.BIM: Building Information Modeling; a digital building model that also helps produce drawings.
glossary.Level 1: The first floor of the house.
glossary.Level 2: The second floor of the house.
glossary.rubric: The scoring guide used to judge the work.
glossary.assessment: The final review of the choices and written answers.
glossary.homeowner: The person who owns the house and is the client for this project.
glossary.pickup: The act of reviewing a redline and deciding what to update or check.
glossary.model: The digital building file the team uses to study and update the design.
glossary.foundation: The part of a building that supports it from below.
glossary.rear addition: A new part of the house added to the back side.
glossary.architectural designer: A person who helps create building designs and drawings under an architect's direction.
glossary.callout: A note or label on a drawing that points to a specific item.
glossary.jargon: Special words used by a job or field that may not be familiar to everyone.
glossary.overpromising: Saying something is certain before the team has checked it.
glossary.residential addition: A new room or building area added to a house.
glossary.second-floor bedroom suite: A bedroom area on the second floor, often with related spaces such as a closet or bathroom.
glossary.field verification: Checking a detail at the real site before using it in the design.
glossary.verification: The act of checking that something is true before using it.

criteria.name: Choosing the first step
criteria.rubric_text: 9-10: The student chooses a careful first step for a morning with many limits. They compare client goals with zoning, redlines, drawing gaps, and questions before acting. Score only this task. 7-8: The main choice is good, but one priority or reason is a little weak. 4-6: Some judgment is useful, but important checks or next steps are missing. 0-3: The choice misses the task, ignores evidence, creates a risky promise, or is not usable.

criteria.name: Sorting drawing changes
criteria.rubric_text: 9-10: The student understands each redline and separates drawing changes that are already proven from items that need checking first. They do not mix up proven tag fixes, such as W-4/W-1 or missing W-10, with items that need verification, such as setback, lot coverage, privacy, and existing site conditions. Score only this task. 7-8: The main sorting is right, with one small gap. 4-6: Some items are sorted correctly, but several important Fix or Verify choices are wrong or unclear. 0-3: The task goal is missed, evidence is ignored, or the result would cause confusion or risky drawing changes.

criteria.name: Choosing client questions
criteria.rubric_text: 9-10: The student chooses two Dana-facing questions that help the design team make choices: one about west-bedroom privacy/daylight and one about kitchen, mudroom, or daily routine priorities. They leave zoning, lot coverage, drawing tags, and field conditions as team checks. Score only this checklist. 7-8: The selected questions are mostly useful, but one is less focused or a team-check distractor is tempting. 4-6: The checklist includes only one useful client choice or mixes client questions with technical checks. 0-3: The selections ask Dana to solve zoning, permit, tag, or field issues instead of homeowner choices.

criteria.name: Creating the design option
criteria.rubric_text: 9-10: The student creates a usable schematic option that fits Dana's goals and the team rules. The option places the needed room labels, uses Translucent glass or privacy glazing for the privacy choice, and clears live blockers. Score only this task. 7-8: The option mostly works, but one detail, priority, or unresolved item is a little weak. 4-6: Some pieces are present, but important design choices or checks are missing. 0-3: The task goal is missed, the rules are ignored, risky promises are made, or the submission is not usable.
